items.question_text: Under 9-3-1, what are the five conditions under which the section's separation procedures do NOT apply to aircraft near SUA, ATCAAs, or stationary ALTRVs?
items.explanation: The five exceptions are: (a) pilot obtained using agency permission, (b) using agency informs ATC of permission, (c) airspace released to controlling agency, (d) aircraft on approved ALTRV (unless ATCAA), and (e) offshore/oceanic operations per Chapter 8. Additionally, alert areas and controlled firing areas are always excluded.

choices.text: Pilot has permission from using agency, using agency informs ATC of permission, airspace released to controlling agency, aircraft on approved ALTRV (except ATCAA), or offshore/oceanic operations.
The airspace is within 30 NM of an airport, the aircraft is IFR, the using agency is military, the altitude is above FL 180, or the airspace is a MOA.
Night operations, VFR weather, pilot request, supervisor approval, and DEN coordination.
Only when the using agency is closed, the airspace is cold, and the pilot has filed a DVFR flight plan.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-1, are Alert Areas and Controlled Firing Areas subject to the separation procedures of this section?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'These procedures are not applicable to Alert Areas or Controlled Firing Areas.'

choices.text: Yes, they are subject to the same separation procedures as Restricted Areas.
No, these procedures are not applicable to Alert Areas or Controlled Firing Areas.
Only Alert Areas; Controlled Firing Areas still require the full separation procedures.
They are subject to the procedures only when they are active.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-2, what altitude separation is provided between nonparticipating aircraft and active SUA, ATCAAs, or stationary ALTRVs?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-2a, assign an altitude at least 500 feet (above FL 290 — 1,000 feet) above or below the upper/lower limit of the airspace.

choices.text: 1,000 feet at all altitudes, regardless of flight level.
At least 500 feet (above FL 290 — 1,000 feet) above or below the upper or lower limit of the airspace.
2,000 feet at all altitudes for all types of SUA.
Only 3 miles laterally; vertical separation is not required for SUA boundaries.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-2, what radar separation is normally provided from the peripheral boundary of SUA, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-2b, provide radar separation of 3 miles (FL 600 and above — 6 miles) from the peripheral boundary. The two exceptions are certain security/hazardous areas and space launch ALTRVs where aircraft must remain clear.

choices.text: 5 miles at all altitudes.
3 miles (6 miles at and above FL 600).
1 mile for all SUA, with no altitude-based variation.
Radar separation from SUA boundaries is not required; only altitude separation applies.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-2, for stationary ALTRVs and TFRs protecting aircraft hazard areas (AHA) during space launch or reentry, what is the rule about nonparticipating aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-2d, do not allow nonparticipating aircraft to operate in an AHA unless real-time notifications of actual start/end of activity are provided via ATO Space Operations coordination. Same applies to ATC services at airports within an AHA.

choices.text: Nonparticipating aircraft may enter freely as long as they maintain 3 miles from the launch vehicle.
Do not allow operation in the AHA unless real-time start/end notifications are provided via ATO Space Operations coordination.
Aircraft may transit AHAs at any time because ALTRV airspace is not restricted airspace.
Only IFR aircraft are restricted; VFR aircraft may freely transit AHAs.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-2, what crossing angle is required for aircraft on approved routing through a Transitional Hazard Area (THA) for space launch or reentry?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-2e, routing approved by ATO Space Operations must have an angular difference of 30 through 150 degrees from the launch/reentry course, or crossing courses of 45 through 135 degrees.

choices.text: Exactly 90 degrees from the launch/reentry course.
30 through 150 degrees (approved routing) or 45 through 135 degrees (crossing courses) from the launch/reentry course.
Any angle is acceptable as long as the aircraft maintains 5 miles separation.
0 through 90 degrees only; angles greater than 90 degrees are not permitted in THAs.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-3, how far above or below the airspace limit must a VFR-on-top aircraft be instructed to fly when its route may enter active SUA or an ATCAA?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-3a, inform the pilot to conduct flight VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit or below the lower limit of the airspace. The 500-foot buffer also applies to routed clearances per 9-3-3b.

choices.text: At least 1,000 feet above or below.
At least 500 feet above or below.
At least 3 miles laterally; no vertical buffer is required.
At least 2,000 feet above or below.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-3, when is the added 500-foot (or 1,000-foot) buffer NOT required for a Prohibited or Restricted Area?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-3c, the exception applies to 'some Prohibited/Restricted Areas established for security reasons or to contain hazardous activities not involving aircraft operations,' when identified by facility management.

choices.text: When the area is established for security reasons or to contain hazardous activities not involving aircraft operations, as identified by facility management.
When the area is a Warning Area located offshore.
When the aircraft is operating under IFR at or above FL 180.
When the pilot has obtained using agency permission to transit the area.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-4, when a LOA/LOP is in place and the using agency has granted permission to transit the area, what does the controller comply with?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-4a, comply with the instructions/clearances issued by the Using Agency and provide applicable separation minima between aircraft when two or more aircraft are transiting the area.

choices.text: The instructions/clearances issued by the Using Agency, providing applicable separation between transiting aircraft.
Only standard ATC separation minima, ignoring any using agency instructions.
The pilot's requested routing through the area without using agency coordination.
A mandatory altitude of at least 5,000 feet above the area's upper limit.

items.question_text: Under 9-3-4, what must a controller do if unable to comply with the using agency's instructions or clearances during a coordinated transit?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-4b, if unable to comply, clear the aircraft in accordance with paragraph 9-3-2, Separation Minima — effectively treating the aircraft as a nonparticipating aircraft and applying standard SUA separation.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft in accordance with paragraph 9-3-2, Separation Minima.
Deny the transit and require the aircraft to circumnavigate the area.
Request the using agency to deactivate the area.
Issue a clearance through the area using standard IFR separation without using agency coordination.

items.situation: An active Restricted Area has an upper limit of FL 230. A nonparticipating IFR aircraft at FL 240 needs to cross directly over the area. Another controller says FL 240 is fine because it's above the limit.
items.question_text: What altitude does the nonparticipating aircraft need to maintain?

choices.text: The aircraft must be at least 500 feet above the upper limit, so it needs FL 235 or higher (or 1,000 feet if above FL 290).
FL 240 is acceptable because any altitude above the area's upper limit is sufficient.
The aircraft must be at least 2,000 feet above the upper limit.
Lateral separation of 3 miles is the only option; overflight is not permitted regardless of altitude.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-2a, nonparticipating aircraft must be assigned an altitude at least 500 feet above (or below) the upper/lower limit of the airspace (1,000 feet above FL 290).

items.question_text: Under 9-3-4, who has jurisdictional authority over the use of nonjoint-use prohibited, restricted, or warning area airspace?

choices.text: The FAA does not have jurisdictional authority; clearance cannot be issued for flight therein without the appropriate approval.
The FAA has full authority over all SUA including nonjoint-use areas.
Jurisdiction is shared equally between the FAA and the using agency.
The pilot-in-command has jurisdictional authority in nonjoint-use areas.

items.explanation: Per the note to 9-3-4, 'The FAA has no jurisdictional authority over the use of nonjoint use prohibited/restricted/warning area airspace; therefore, clearance cannot be issued for flight therein without the appropriate approval.'
